title: Amigos

jokes.text: Gob's motivational poster reads 'Don't be afraid to make a mi-' — cut off mid-word
jokes.setup: Michael arrives to find Gob 'adjusting to his new job as president' — we expect a presidential, motivational atmosphere
jokes.punchline: The motivational poster is cut off mid-word ('mi-'), implying 'mistake' — Gob literally couldn't even finish making a motivational poster about not being afraid to make mistakes
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical visual gag. The poster IS the joke — Gob failed at the thing the poster was supposed to encourage. Perfect character integration (only Gob would do this), perfect structure (the setup is the punchline), and the narrator's deadpan 'I'm not gonna beat myself up over that' adds a second layer. High rewatch value because you have to catch the cut-off text.

jokes.text: Gob: 'My legs are so powerful.'
jokes.setup: Gob has just knocked something over, presumably with his legs
jokes.punchline: Rather than apologizing, Gob brags about the power of his legs as if that's the takeaway from clumsily destroying something
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob. His narcissism is so total that breaking something with his legs becomes evidence of their power rather than clumsiness. The line is short, quotable, and entirely character-specific. A lesser show would have him apologize.

jokes.text: Gob reads 'circumvrent' then 'circumverate' — Michael corrects: 'Circumvent.' Gob: 'The old reach around.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to read the word 'circumvent' on a shareholders' statement he's supposed to have written
jokes.punchline: After two failed attempts at the word, he defines it himself as 'the old reach around' — a sexually crude euphemism delivered with total confidence as if it's the same thing
jokes.explanation: Three-part escalation: wrong word, wronger word, then leaps to crude sexual slang delivered with utter confidence. The escalation in wrongness is perfectly structured. 'The old reach around' is Gob reframing his ignorance as expertise — peak character comedy. Highly quotable and genuinely shocking in context.

jokes.text: Starla brings photo-sized photocopies. 'When you said you wanted photocopies, I thought you meant you wanted them on a photo printer.' Michael: 'Tell you what. From now on, we're gonna use just the big printer.' Starla: 'Okay. You're a perfectionist.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks for copies of the shareholders' statement; Starla returns with tiny photo-printer versions
jokes.punchline: Starla interprets Michael's completely reasonable correction as perfectionism, implying she thinks the tiny copies were fine
jokes.explanation: The visual of comically tiny photocopies is strong, but the real joke is Starla's complete reframing of a reasonable request as Michael being demanding. The 'you're a perfectionist' line is a great character beat — she's not stupid, she's operating on a completely different wavelength.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Flashes of Quincy. Starla claimed to have had a relationship with music legend Quincy Jones.'
jokes.setup: Starla says 'You're a perfectionist. Flashes of Quincy' — an offhand remark
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals that 'Quincy' refers to Quincy Jones, and that Starla claims a personal relationship with him — treating an absurd non-sequitur with documentary seriousness
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan explication of Starla's bizarre claim is a reliable AD formula — the comedy comes from the show treating the ridiculous as documentary fact. The Quincy Jones detail is delightfully specific and random in a way that feels earned rather than cheap.

jokes.text: Gob: 'No problem. I'll just call up some of my friends.' Michael and Lindsay beat: 'What?' / 'What friends?'
jokes.setup: Michael says the LEGOLAND trip is just for him and his son; Gob cheerfully says he'll call his friends
jokes.punchline: Both Michael and Lindsay simultaneously challenge the existence of Gob's friends — establishing it as a known fact among the family
jokes.explanation: Plants the 'Gob has no friends' running gag that pays off beautifully throughout the episode. The simultaneous 'What? / What friends?' reaction from two different characters signals it's established family knowledge. Economy is excellent — sets up an entire episode arc in three words.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I've got friends, Michael. I just don't talk to them because... I work all the time.' [long pause]
jokes.setup: Gob defensively claims he has friends; he needs a reason why they never see each other
jokes.punchline: The long pause before 'I work all the time' signals the audience (and Gob) knows this isn't true — the pause is the real punchline
jokes.explanation: The pause does the heavy lifting. The excuse ('I work all the time') is patently absurd for Gob, a man who does magic shows on a boat. The structure relies on the gap between what's said and what everyone knows to be true. Character-specific and well-timed.

jokes.text: Gob, in Michael's old office, staring into the distance: 'I think the plant lady's wearing a thong.'
jokes.setup: Gob has been given the 'president' office as a front; the scene cuts to him sitting in authority
jokes.punchline: His presidential observation is about the plant lady's underwear — a complete non-sequitur that deflates any sense of him growing into the role
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob moment. Positioned as president in a serious office, his only observation is voyeuristic and trivial. The non-sequitur nature and the total confidence with which he delivers it makes it funny. Very short and punchy.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Allow me. I'll just circumvrent you.'
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael are discussing keeping Gob from having real power; Lucille interjects
jokes.punchline: Lucille uses Gob's mangled version of the word ('circumvrent' instead of 'circumvent') — either she picked it up from Gob or she's mocking him
jokes.explanation: Quick callback that rewards attentive viewers. Lucille deploying Gob's malapropism — whether deliberately mocking him or unconsciously absorbing it — is both funny and character-specific. AD's density on display.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I hired him a hundred years ago to find out if your father was cheating on me. He never did find anything.' Michael: 'Well, he can't be very good then.'
jokes.setup: Lucille brags that Gene Parmesan is their private eye, hired to find out if George Sr. was cheating
jokes.punchline: Michael's quip implies George Sr. definitely was cheating — the P.I.'s failure to find evidence is evidence of his incompetence, not George's fidelity
jokes.explanation: Layered joke: it's dark (George cheated on Lucille), it's a great burn on the P.I.'s competence, and it characterizes George Sr. efficiently. Michael delivers it with dry wit. The logic is airtight — absence of evidence is evidence of incompetence, not innocence, given everything we know about George Sr.

jokes.text: Gene Parmesan's entrance: 'Don't get up. I just a-find-a the supplies... because I'm a- Private detective.' [Gene appears from hiding in the office]
jokes.setup: Lucille says Gene found something and wants to tell them in person; they're in Michael's office waiting
jokes.punchline: Gene has been hidden in the office the whole time, emerging from behind the supply area doing a bad Italian accent as cover — his reveal is both his entrance and a demonstration of his 'skills'
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Gene's character — he thinks this kind of theatrical reveal demonstrates his detective chops, but it's just slightly embarrassing. The terrible accent and the 'a-find-a the supplies' is a nice touch. Establishes his persona quickly.

jokes.text: Lucille's reaction to Gene Parmesan: 'Gene! Oh, Gene!' — followed by narrator: 'Gene was far from the best.'
jokes.setup: Michael skeptically says Gene 'can't be very good'; Gene does his theatrical reveal; Lucille reacts with breathless excitement
jokes.punchline: Narrator flatly contradicts Lucille's ecstatic reaction: 'Gene was far from the best' — the juxtaposition of her response with objective reality is the joke
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan undercutting of Lucille's enthusiasm is a classic AD move, but it's executed perfectly here. Lucille's outsized reaction (she's practically in love with Gene) juxtaposed with 'Gene was far from the best' is both funny and character-revealing. Economy is superb.

jokes.text: Michael: 'If I could find Dad, I'd be off the hook. I could be president again.' ... Gene: 'Mexico.' Gob: 'You get the country for free. The city — that's gonna cost ya.'
jokes.setup: Gene says the stair car is in Mexico; the group processes the information
jokes.punchline: Gob offers his 'pricing' for Mexico — the country is free (it's a country, you can't charge for it) but 'the city' costs extra, as if he's a tour guide charging for access to a real place
jokes.explanation: Absurd Gob logic delivered with total confidence. He's pricing geographic regions as if he has jurisdiction over them. The distinction between 'the country' and 'the city' is perfectly nonsensical. Character-specific and quotable.

jokes.text: Gob: 'It's a $200 fine. Oh! I feel like such an-' [Gene cuts him off] 'The car is in a town called Encanta.'
jokes.setup: Gob is about to call himself an idiot (presumably) for mispricing Mexico
jokes.punchline: The sentence is cut off by Gene's information — we never hear what Gob was going to call himself, but the implication is clear and the interruption is funnier than the completed insult would have been
jokes.explanation: AD frequently uses the interrupted self-insult — the absence of the word is funnier than saying it. The timing of Gene's interruption is the craft here. Not the most original move but well-executed.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'You know, he's doing it [joining the army] just to spite me.' Michael: 'Then why are you throwing him a party?' Lucille: 'Just to spite him.' Michael: 'You guys have such a healthy thing going.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is upset that Buster is joining the army
jokes.punchline: Her counter-spite (throwing a party to spite him for spiting her) is perfectly dysfunctional; Michael's deadpan 'healthy thing going' comment nails the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Exceptional character comedy. The Lucille/Buster spite dynamic is reduced to its pure essence in four lines. 'Just to spite him' is a perfectly absurd non-answer that is also completely in character. Michael's 'healthy thing' sarcasm is restrained and sharp. This is what the Bluth family dysfunction looks like when it's perfectly distilled.

jokes.text: Gob, inspired by his mother's private detective, decides to hire someone to follow Michael. Narrator: 'He decided to hire someone to keep an eye on his brother.' Gob: 'I think I might have someone who's going to cirsumvrent the law.'
jokes.setup: The narrator explains Gob's plan; Gob speaks his plan aloud
jokes.punchline: Gob uses yet ANOTHER mangled version of 'circumvent' — this time 'cirsumvrent' — a third variation that confirms he will never learn the word
jokes.explanation: Third iteration of the circumvent gag, and each mangling is different — showing that Gob isn't just stuck on one wrong version but is generating new wrong versions each time. That detail elevates it from a simple repeated joke to a character study in consistent incompetence.

jokes.text: Michael mentions Ann; George Michael shows a photo. Michael: 'I guess I thought she was with another family.' George Michael: 'She's kind of my girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: George Michael shows Michael a photo of himself and Ann in front of a bagel place
jokes.punchline: Michael's explanation for not recognizing Ann — 'I thought she was with another family' — implies he's literally never noticed her as a person, which is both an insult and a confession of obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Establishes the 'Ann is forgettable/invisible' running gag. 'I thought she was with another family' is a perfectly calibrated insult — not mean-spirited, just genuinely oblivious. Sets up a rich vein of comedy throughout the episode and series.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It's like you're hoggin' her — like you're a little Ann hog. Okay? Don't be such an Ann hog.'
jokes.setup: Michael is upset that George Michael spends all his time with Ann instead of with his father
jokes.punchline: Michael coins 'Ann hog' — a portmanteau of 'Ann' and 'road hog' (or 'ball hog') — delivered with increasing conviction as if it's a totally reasonable phrase
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable coinage. 'Ann hog' is a perfect nonsense compound that captures Michael's jealousy and his dismissiveness toward Ann simultaneously. The repetition ('like you're a little Ann hog. Don't be such an Ann hog') commits to the bit. Plants what will become a callback ('Ann Hog's coming?').

jokes.text: 'And 8:01. Curtain has risen.' — Michael checks his watch as soon as he asks Tobias if he's free
jokes.setup: Michael asks Tobias if he's available to drive to Mexico tomorrow; Tobias says he has two shows on standby for the Blue Man Group
jokes.punchline: Michael checks his watch at exactly 8:01 — pre-knowing that Tobias would be unavailable because he's always on standby and always waiting for the curtain
jokes.explanation: Tobias's Blue Man Group standby obsession established here. '8:01. Curtain has risen' is a nice Tobias-specific absurdity — his life is organized around an audition that may never materialize.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Might as well keep this makeup on since it takes so long to get off.' — Tobias is covered in blue paint
jokes.setup: Tobias has been putting on Blue Man Group makeup in anticipation of the show
jokes.punchline: He's fully painted blue and plans to stay that way — the visual of a fully blue Tobias wandering through domestic life is the joke, and it's the beginning of a season-long running gag
jokes.explanation: The beginning of Tobias's iconic 'never-nude covered in blue paint' era. The visual is doing most of the work. Rewatch value is enormous because viewers knowing where this goes makes the innocence of 'might as well keep this on' funnier in retrospect.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'm sorry. I forgot. Your wife is dead.' [long pause]
jokes.setup: Michael is giving Lindsay marital advice about her open marriage; she retorts by pointing out his dead wife
jokes.punchline: The bluntness of 'Your wife is dead' — delivered without softening — followed by a pause that makes the cringe even worse
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best moments. The economy is perfect — five words that devastate an argument. Only Lindsay would deploy her dead sister-in-law as a debate tactic without a second thought. The pause after the line (the awkward silence is the bonus punchline) makes it even more effective. Dark, shocking, perfectly character-specific. Lindsay's immediate profuse apology ('I'm sorry. That was 100% inappropriate') makes it funnier because she KNOWS she went too far.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'm sorry. That was 100% inappropriate, and I do apologize profusely.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just said 'Your wife is dead' to Michael
jokes.punchline: She immediately over-apologizes with formal, legalistic language ('100% inappropriate,' 'apologize profusely') — the stiffness of the apology makes it feel less sincere, not more
jokes.explanation: The follow-up to joke #22. The over-formal, quantified apology ('100%') reveals Lindsay's discomfort but also her self-absorption — she's more concerned with being seen as apologetic than with Michael's feelings. Good extension of the moment.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael and Gob had been to Mexico once before. Chiclet? Chiclet?' / 'You're the Chiclet! Not me!' — Narrator: 'Unfortunately, in Mexican culture, this was a much more inflammatory gesture.'
jokes.setup: Michael considers taking Gob to Mexico; the narrator references a previous trip
jokes.punchline: The flashback shows what appears to be an innocuous chiclet-selling interaction that turned violent because of some gesture — the narrator's understatement ('a much more inflammatory gesture') is funnier for what it doesn't explain
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of the narrator to imply more than is shown. 'This was a much more inflammatory gesture' tells you something bad happened without showing it — the audience's imagination fills in the blank. 'You're the Chiclet! Not me!' is a bizarre enough line to suggest something truly unhinged preceded it.

jokes.text: Lucille giving Buster a camcorder: 'That's so you can videotape it when they put you in a naked pyramid and point to your Charlie Browns.'
jokes.setup: Buster's send-off party; Lucille gives him a gift
jokes.punchline: The gift is a camcorder — ostensibly sweet — but Lucille's stated purpose is to capture Abu Ghraib-style military abuse, specifically mentioning 'naked pyramid' and 'Charlie Browns' (genitalia euphemism) as if this is a reasonable parting thought
jokes.explanation: Lucille's casual mention of Abu Ghraib-style abuse (referencing a real 2003-2004 scandal) as the obvious use case for a parting gift is brilliantly dark and specific. 'Charlie Browns' as a euphemism for genitals adds a layer of absurd propriety to an already inappropriate statement. The contrast between the warmth of a gift and the horror of her intent is perfectly Lucille.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I ought to point to Uncle Oscar's Charlie Browns next time you're on top of him, Mother.'
jokes.setup: Lucille makes the naked pyramid/Charlie Browns joke; Buster retaliates
jokes.punchline: Buster accuses Lucille of having sex with Oscar (his uncle/possible biological father) — a devastating family secret deployed as a childish comeback, using her own 'Charlie Browns' language against her
jokes.explanation: Buster weaponizing Lucille's own cutesy language ('Charlie Browns') against her while revealing the Oscar affair is a beautifully constructed escalation. The fact that Buster says it with the petulant energy of a child while referencing his mother's adultery makes it both funnier and more uncomfortable. Rewatch value because the Oscar paternity storyline pays off much later.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Just because he thinks I'm having an affair with the boy's uncle.' / Narrator: 'Yes. And she's not having an affair with the boy's... uncle.'
jokes.setup: Lucille defensively explains why Buster is angry with her
jokes.punchline: The narrator's pause before 'uncle' — 'she's not having an affair with the boy's... uncle' — implies she IS having an affair, and the pause around 'uncle' suggests Oscar's status as uncle is itself complicated (he may be Buster's biological father)
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's cleverest moments. The narrator confirming 'she's not having an affair' while the ellipsis before 'uncle' does all the work — implying Oscar may not technically be 'the boy's uncle' if he's actually the boy's father. The joke operates on two levels: she IS having an affair (irony) and even the word 'uncle' is in question. Dense, layered, high rewatch value.

jokes.text: Buster dramatically declares 'It's too late for apologies. I'm going to the army.' — then immediately: 'Get me out. You've got to get me out.'
jokes.setup: Buster has been making dramatic statements about joining the army to punish Lucille
jokes.punchline: His grand martyrdom speech ('It's too late for apologies. I'm going to the army') is immediately undercut by desperate private whisper to Michael: 'Get me out. You've got to get me out.'
jokes.explanation: The structural perfection here is remarkable — Buster's public bravado immediately collapsing into private panic is exquisitely timed. The gap between performance and reality is Buster's entire character in two lines. 'Get me out. You've got to get me out.' is instantly quotable and lands with maximum impact because of its proximity to the speech. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Blueprint of George Sr.'s face printed on the big printer — 'She used the big printer. I'll give her that.'
jokes.setup: Michael asked Starla to print a photo of George Sr. to show people in Mexico; she delivers a blueprint-sized print of his face
jokes.punchline: Starla, corrected about using the big printer, now uses it for EVERYTHING — including a comically oversized face 'blueprint.' Michael can only muster 'I'll give her that.'
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback escalation. Starla took Michael's correction to an absurd extreme — a blueprint of a face. Michael's defeated 'I'll give her that' is a beautifully resigned acknowledgment that this is the best he's going to get. Visual gag inferred from 'blueprint of Dad's face.'

jokes.text: Ice introduced as Gob's 'friend' on the couch. 'Ice may be my best friend.' Narrator: 'In fact, the man was Ice, a bounty hunter that Gob hired to follow Michael.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Michael notice a strange man on the couch; Gob claims he's his friend
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Ice is a paid bounty hunter — Gob is calling a contractor he hired to surveil his brother his 'best friend,' because he has no actual friends
jokes.explanation: The 'Gob has no friends' arc reaches a perfect midpoint here. He's so starved for connection that he's decided a hired bounty hunter is his best friend. 'Ice may be my best friend' is a heartbreaking/hilarious line that perfectly captures Gob's emotional state through a comedic lens.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Ann Hog's coming? I thought it was just the two of us.' Michael: 'Just Ann.'
jokes.setup: Michael said Ann could come; George Michael confirms
jokes.punchline: George Michael uses 'Ann Hog' as if it's Ann's name — having absorbed Michael's earlier coinage — and Michael has to correct him: 'Just Ann'
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — George Michael has already internalized 'Ann Hog' as a name, and Michael's 'Just Ann' correction is both funny and ironic (he coined the nickname). The exchange is two lines and does a lot of work.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I'm gonna have to start putting people in the trunk.' Narrator: 'In fact, at that moment, Buster was climbing into the trunk.'
jokes.setup: The car is getting crowded; Michael jokes about trunks
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Buster is literally in the trunk at that moment — Michael's hyperbolic complaint is immediately literalized
jokes.explanation: The structure here is near-perfect. Michael's throwaway hyperbolic joke is immediately revealed to be literally true by the narrator. The timing (the narrator cuts in immediately after Michael's line) makes this a textbook example of AD's signature move: the joke is in the juxtaposition between character statement and narrator revelation. High impact because the reveal is genuinely surprising.

jokes.text: Buster records a farewell message in the trunk: 'Mother, when you see this videotape, you'll know that I left. Not out of cowardice, but out of—' [pause] 'Oh, man. It's tiring in here.'
jokes.setup: Buster is making a dramatic farewell video message in the trunk of the car
jokes.punchline: His heroic speech deflates immediately when he notices how tiring it is to be in a trunk — the grandiosity collapses into petty physical complaint
jokes.explanation: Classic Buster. He begins with genuine emotional stakes ('not out of cowardice, but out of—') and immediately crumbles into childlike complaint ('It's tiring in here'). The interruption of his own heroic narrative by physical discomfort is perfect. Only Buster would abandon a farewell speech to note that trunks are tiring.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Six minutes later, Michael dropped off his mother's housekeeper.' / Buster emerges: 'Mexico.' / Narrator: 'In fact, he was in Santa Ana, a town six minutes inland from his own.'
jokes.setup: Buster has snuck into the trunk to escape to Mexico
jokes.punchline: He's only traveled six minutes — to Santa Ana, near home — but he emerges declaring 'Mexico' with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great structural jokes. The setup (Buster in trunk going to Mexico) is subverted perfectly — he got out six minutes later at a housekeeper's stop. His triumphant 'Mexico' is completely, hilariously wrong. The narrator's clinical delivery of 'he was in Santa Ana, a town six minutes inland' is pitch-perfect. Character-specific (only Buster would have no idea how long Mexico takes to reach) and beautifully timed.

jokes.text: Buster in Santa Ana, holding a sombrero: 'Oh, this shall keep me safe from the hot Mexican sun.'
jokes.setup: Buster has 'arrived in Mexico' (actually Santa Ana) and is adapting to his new life
jokes.punchline: He holds up a found sombrero with total sincerity — the visual of Buster adopting Mexican cultural markers in what is essentially his hometown is the joke
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Buster's complete commitment to the fiction. 'This shall keep me safe' is wonderfully archaic language for a man protecting himself from imaginary Mexican sun. The visual of him holding a sombrero with gravitas is perfect Buster.

jokes.text: Ice reports to Gob: 'Still on his tail, about 20 miles from LEGOLAND.' Gob: 'Go get him, friend.' Ice: 'You're not my friend. You're a client.'
jokes.setup: Gob is on the phone with Ice, who is tracking Michael
jokes.punchline: Gob calls Ice 'friend'; Ice flatly corrects him — 'You're not my friend. You're a client.' — and Gob's desperate bid for connection is shot down by the man he hired to be his friend
jokes.explanation: The 'Gob has no friends' arc hits its peak emotional beat here. Ice's matter-of-fact 'You're not my friend. You're a client' is devastating in its simplicity. The bounty hunter has clearer professional boundaries than Gob has emotional ones. Highly quotable. The narrator's follow-up ('Gob realized that perhaps he really didn't have any friends') makes it even better.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Gob realized that perhaps he really didn't have any friends.'
jokes.setup: Ice has just told Gob 'You're not my friend. You're a client.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'perhaps' — as if this is a new and uncertain realization, when the audience has known it for the whole episode — is the perfect understated kicker
jokes.explanation: 'Perhaps' is doing enormous work here. The hedging of a complete certainty (Gob has no friends) as if it's an open question the narrator is reluctant to commit to is a perfect comedy note. Short, precise, and perfectly timed after Ice's blunt correction.

jokes.text: Oscar: 'Here's some money. Go see a Star War.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is trying to get Lucille alone; he hands George Michael (or someone) money to leave
jokes.punchline: 'A Star War' — not 'Star Wars' — delivered as if this is a normal thing to say. The singularization is either a generational gag or a character-specific quirk
jokes.explanation: Four words with maximum impact. 'A Star War' is a perfectly chosen malapropism — the kind an out-of-touch stoner hippie character would deploy. The indefinite article transforms it from a franchise reference to an absurd singular event. Incredibly quotable for such a small moment.

jokes.text: Lucille calls Gene Parmesan to spy on Gob spying on Michael. Gene is already hiding nearby and answers her call.
jokes.setup: Lucille is suspicious of Gob's motives; she calls her P.I. to spy on Gob
jokes.punchline: Gene is already hidden somewhere near the building — he's always there, always ready, always lurking. The spy-within-a-spy setup (Gob hired a bounty hunter to follow Michael; Lucille hires the P.I. to follow Gob) is an absurd escalating surveillance nest
jokes.explanation: The nested surveillance structure (Michael→Ice→Gob→Gene→Lucille) is a clever absurdist escalation. Gene's immediate proximity when Lucille calls is a recurring joke about his omnipresent lurking. Good structural comedy.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Gene?' / Narrator: 'It's just some idiot with balloons.' / Lucille: 'Oh, is it?' / Gob appears with balloons. Lucille: 'I knew it!'
jokes.setup: Lucille is on the phone with Gene; her doorbell rings; Gene says it's 'just some idiot with balloons'
jokes.punchline: Lucille immediately knows it's Gob — 'Oh, is it? I knew it!' — treating 'idiot with balloons' as a sufficient description to identify her son
jokes.explanation: Gene's descriptor 'some idiot with balloons' is both an insult and an exact identification — Lucille needs no further information. The implication that 'idiot with balloons' is uniquely Gob's signature is a perfect economy of character establishment. Lucille's 'I knew it!' is triumphant in the most dysfunctional way.

jokes.text: Buster in Santa Ana discovers Lupe's home has his old shirt and his childhood hand chair. 'I never thought I'd miss a hand so much.' / 'You people are just like me.'
jokes.setup: Buster thinks he's in Mexico and is discovering the local culture; he keeps finding his own childhood possessions
jokes.punchline: 'You people are just like me' — he thinks he's having a cross-cultural bonding moment but he's literally just surrounded by his own donated belongings. The hand chair line foreshadows his later hand loss.
jokes.explanation: Rich with layers. 'I never thought I'd miss a hand so much' is a foreshadowing plant for Buster's eventual hook-hand storyline (major rewatch bonus). 'You people are just like me' is the perfect punch — his cross-cultural revelation is just him recognizing his own stuff. Dark, brilliant, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Michael finds the stair car in Mexico. Mexican workers interpret the blueprint of George Sr.'s face as construction plans. Worker: 'Oh, yeah. Sure, señor. No problem. When do you want us to start building?'
jokes.setup: Michael is showing the blueprint-sized printout of George Sr.'s face to locals to ask if they've seen him
jokes.punchline: The workers think the face blueprint is an actual construction blueprint and offer to start building 'it'
jokes.explanation: The Starla printer callback pays off in a completely unexpected way. Starla's absurd oversized print has a real-world consequence: it looks like a construction blueprint. 'When do you want us to start building?' is the perfect absurdist escalation. The whole chain (Starla's tiny copies → 'use the big printer' → blueprint face → construction misunderstanding) is a master class in callback comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr. is in a church in Mexico, being mourned by Kitty — he's been declared dead.
jokes.setup: Michael is searching the church for his father
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is inside being mourned — he's literally there but declared dead, in a church, with a mourner. The comedy is in the mundane absurdity of the dead man attending his own mourning
jokes.explanation: The situation is inherently funny (legally dead man being mourned while alive) and the Kitty detail adds color — she's mourning her boss/lover. Structural irony that the son searching for his father is literally separated from him by the church door.

jokes.text: Ice stops Michael. Michael: 'Gene? There's no way you're that good.' Ice: 'Name's Ice. Gob hired me to stop you from fleeing the country.'
jokes.setup: Ice appears; Michael mistakes him for Gene Parmesan
jokes.punchline: Michael's reaction ('There's no way you're that good') — insulting Gene's competence by assuming the person who found him in Mexico must be someone else — reveals how low the bar is for Gene
jokes.explanation: Michael's kneejerk disbelief that Gene could have found him is a good character beat. The joke layers Ice's reveal with another Gene insult. Clean and efficient.

jokes.text: Michael tries to show Ice a photo of George Sr.: 'No, this — That's actually him. He's just — He's much smaller in real life.' Ice: 'I can see that's a man.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to redirect Ice to find George Sr. instead; he shows the blueprint-sized face printout
jokes.punchline: Ice has to specify 'I can see that's a man' — because the printout is so large and so blueprint-like that clarification is apparently necessary
jokes.explanation: Third beat of the blueprint gag. Ice's deadpan 'I can see that's a man' suggests the printout is confusing enough that confirming humanity is a reasonable first step. The escalating absurdity of this printout causing more and more confusion is a great running visual gag.

jokes.text: Ann: 'What did he say? Did he ask about me?' [re: Ice, the bounty hunter]
jokes.setup: Ice has just revealed he's a bounty hunter looking for George Sr.; George Michael relays info to Ann
jokes.punchline: Ann's only concern is whether the professional bounty hunter asked about her — complete romantic obliviousness to the situation's severity
jokes.explanation: Ann's teenage romantic self-centeredness in a crisis situation is funny and character-consistent. Her complete misreading of the bounty hunter interaction (did he NOTICE me?) is a good character comedy beat.

jokes.text: George Michael answers his phone in front of Michael: 'Oh, hi, Dad. Yeah. They think you're in Mexico. I'll see you when I get home.'
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael are in Mexico actively searching for George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Michael casually takes a call from George Sr. right in front of Michael — 'They think you're in Mexico' (they ARE in Mexico) — and gives away their location/status while Michael is right there
jokes.explanation: The oblivious phone call to a fugitive grandfather right in front of the searching father is delightfully absurd. 'They think you're in Mexico' — spoken in Mexico, to the man they're looking for, while standing next to him — is perfect dramatic irony. George Michael's naivety is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: George Michael (to grandfather on phone): 'Ye— Oh, yes. Thank you. No, I found my birth control pills. Thanks for asking, Dad.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is wrapping up the call with George Sr.
jokes.punchline: He lies (or the grandfather asks something bizarre) and says he found his birth control pills — a teenager speaking to his grandfather about contraceptives, casually, in front of his father
jokes.explanation: A completely unexpected twist in a scene that was already funny. George Michael's casual mention of birth control pills on the phone — directed at his grandfather — is a layered absurdity: what is a teenage boy doing with birth control pills? Why is Grandpa George asking? Why is George Michael comfortable saying this in front of Michael? Every question the viewer asks makes it funnier. Highly quotable and weird in the best possible way.

jokes.text: Michael and Maeby conversation: Michael thinks they're talking about Ann; Maeby thinks they're talking about Lindsay. Narrator: 'At no point were Michael and Maeby talking about the same person.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he'll go back with Maeby; both begin venting about someone they're each sick of
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals they were talking about completely different people the entire time — and the lines work perfectly for both interpretations
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed dual-meaning dialogue. Every line Michael says about Ann (she's a child, wasting his time, the lame flirting) also applies to Lindsay from Maeby's perspective. The narrator's reveal — 'at no point were Michael and Maeby talking about the same person' — is a perfect button. 'And it's not a race thing' adds a third layer of absurdity. This is sophisticated comedy writing.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And it's not a race thing.' — as punctuation on the Michael/Maeby miscommunication
jokes.setup: The narrator has just explained that Michael and Maeby were not talking about the same person
jokes.punchline: 'And it's not a race thing' — an unprompted clarification that actually raises the question of whether there's a racial dimension, by denying it so emphatically
jokes.explanation: Five words that do enormous work. The narrator's insistence that the miscommunication is 'not a race thing' raises the possibility by denying it — classic comedic denial logic. The intrusion of social sensitivity into a show about wealthy WASP criminals is itself funny. Economy score near-perfect.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Unfortunately, this left the girl Michael was determined to get to know alone in Mexico.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just arranged to ride back with Maeby, sending George Michael back with Lindsay — and apparently forgetting Ann
jokes.punchline: Ann has been left alone in Mexico — the girl Michael specifically said he wanted to get to know has been abandoned. The narrator's 'unfortunately' is perfectly dry.
jokes.explanation: The punchline of the whole Ann subplot — Michael gets so distracted with his cross-purposes conversation with Maeby that he literally leaves his son's girlfriend in Mexico. The narrator's 'determined to get to know' callback to Michael's stated goal makes the failure funnier.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I love being Mexican.' Lupe's family: 'We must work now, amigo. Adiós.' Buster: 'I am one of you now. Ssí?'
jokes.setup: Buster has fully committed to his new 'Mexican' identity in what is actually Santa Ana, California
jokes.punchline: 'I am one of you now. Ssí?' — Buster's phonetic attempt at 'sí' ('yes' in Spanish) mispronounced with an extra 's' and a questioning inflection, as if he's asking permission to be Mexican
jokes.explanation: Buster's oblivious cultural appropriation in what is actually his own country is already funny; his mispronounced 'Ssí?' as a request for validation from people he's decided are his people is the perfect kicker. Character-specific, quotable, and the questioning inflection on 'Ssí?' is a great detail.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Yeah. We won.' [Re: the drive back from Mexico]
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives home before Michael and is asked if they had a good talk
jokes.punchline: 'We won' — treating the drive home from Mexico as a race, despite that being completely incongruous with the situation
jokes.explanation: A small, good character beat. 'We won' suggests George Michael and Ann treated the drive back as a race — endearingly weird teenage behavior. Economy is great (two words) but it's a quick chuckle rather than a sustained joke.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That cousin of yours is a hell of a girl. Too bad you can't date her.' / George Michael: 'No. I meant — Wait, you wouldn't have a problem with that?'
jokes.setup: Michael is talking about Maeby (his niece); George Michael has a crush on Maeby (his first cousin)
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'Wait, you wouldn't have a problem with that?' reveals he's been thinking about dating Maeby and takes Michael's 'too bad' as a potential green light
jokes.explanation: Perfectly handled. Michael's 'too bad you can't date her' is a throwaway social nicety (meaning she's a great catch but she's family). George Michael's response — hoping 'you wouldn't have a problem with that?' — is both alarming and completely in character. The reveal that he's seriously considering it is dark comedy gold. Rewatch value because it's a major running storyline.

jokes.text: Michael realizes he left Ann in Mexico. Long pause. 'She's gonna be fine. We just gotta beat the sun.'
jokes.setup: George Michael asks what Michael thought of Ann; Michael realizes Ann was never in the car
jokes.punchline: The pause of horror, followed by Michael's completely unreassuring 'She's gonna be fine. We just gotta beat the sun' — as if the sun is the main threat to an American teenager left alone in Mexico
jokes.explanation: The moment of realization is perfectly timed, and 'She's gonna be fine. We just gotta beat the sun' is a brilliantly deflecting non-answer. Michael's priorities are clear: the sun is a bigger problem than a teenager abandoned in a foreign country. The pause is the first punchline; the reassurance is the second. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Hey. You think I'm happy about this? But, hey, we are getting that trip together we wanted, huh? And look. Two exits to LEGOLAND.'
jokes.setup: Michael is rushing back to Mexico to retrieve Ann, his son's abandoned girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Michael tries to silver-lining the crisis by noting they're near LEGOLAND — the destination he originally wanted to take George Michael. Checking in on the LEGOLAND counter while someone is stranded in Mexico is peak Michael self-absorption.
jokes.explanation: The LEGOLAND callback in the middle of an Ann rescue is a good character beat. Michael can't help but note proximity to his preferred activity even during a crisis. The running LEGOLAND sign gag (20 miles, then 2 exits) is a nice throughline.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I figured if I blued myself early, I'd be nice and relaxed for a 9:00 dinner reservation.'
jokes.setup: Gob is applying Blue Man Group makeup; he explains his timing logic
jokes.punchline: 'Blued myself' — a callback to Tobias's famous 'I've made a huge mistake' / 'I just blue myself' Arrested Development running gag. Gob using 'blued myself' in reference to his own Blue Man Group audition prep creates a cross-character joke.
jokes.explanation: One of the series' most beloved running gags. 'I blued myself' works simultaneously as a genuine statement (I applied blue makeup) and as an Arrested Development wink (the phrase 'blue myself' echoing Tobias's season 1 'I just blue myself' gag). Highly quotable, rewatch bonus for catching the layered reference.

jokes.text: Ice is lurking outside, startles Gob. 'What are you doing? You're gonna scare away the Ice.' Gob: 'Ice? Ice is out there?'
jokes.setup: Gob is preparing for dinner; discovers Ice is still outside surveillance
jokes.punchline: Gob is alarmed that Ice is outside — as if forgetting he hired him. The surveillance loop (Gob hired Ice, Lucille hired Gene to watch Gob) has become so complicated that Gob doesn't know where his own bounty hunter is
jokes.explanation: A serviceable scene transition joke. Gob being alarmed by his own employee is consistent with his character but not especially inspired. Sets up the George Sr. mistaken-identity scene.

jokes.text: Ice tackles a man, declares 'George Bluth, you're coming with me.' The man is George Michael's grandfather (Oscar? or wrong person entirely). 'It's an honest mistake. Also, I think your knee is on my heart.'
jokes.setup: Ice is using Michael's blueprint-face photo to find George Sr.; he spots someone who matches
jokes.punchline: Ice has tackled the wrong person — and the person's complaint is not 'you have the wrong man' but 'I think your knee is on my heart,' a medically alarming understatement delivered with complete calm
jokes.explanation: The blueprint-face gag pays off with physical consequences. Ice tackling the wrong man because of the terrible photo is perfectly logical. 'I think your knee is on my heart' is a deadpan gem — the wrong person is more concerned about a knee on a vital organ than being misidentified. The callback chain is complete.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'He's my husband. My father is probably still in Mexico.' Ice: 'I was just hoping to have sex with you.' Man on ground: 'Why am I not blacking out?'
jokes.setup: Ice has tackled the wrong man; Lucille clarifies who George Sr. is
jokes.punchline: Three simultaneous non-sequiturs: Lucille correctly identifies her husband and father (suggesting the wrong-person is her husband, and George Sr. is her father? Or mixed-up speech?); Ice confesses he was hoping for a sexual encounter with Lucille; the tackled man wonders why he hasn't lost consciousness from knee-on-heart
jokes.explanation: The scene erupts into three simultaneous absurdist threads. Ice's 'I was just hoping to have sex with you' is a bizarre non-sequitur confession. The tackled man's 'Why am I not blacking out?' — wondering why he's still conscious with a knee on his heart — is a perfectly delivered medical absurdity. Three jokes in one beat.

jokes.text: Gob: 'He set me up. Michael set me up.'
jokes.setup: The chaos of Ice's mistake is unfolding; Gob processes what happened
jokes.punchline: Gob's paranoid conclusion — that Michael orchestrated the bounty hunter confusion — is completely delusional, but from Gob's perspective it's logical given his own suspicions
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat. Gob's paranoia is consistent, but this is a weaker joke — more of a plot continuation than a comedy standout. Sets up the Mexico confrontation scene.

jokes.text: Buster at a dishwasher job: 'This is great. We're like slave buddies.'
jokes.setup: Buster has integrated into 'Mexican' life (actually Santa Ana) and gotten a dishwasher job
jokes.punchline: 'Slave buddies' — Buster cheerfully describes exploitative labor conditions as a bonding experience, with complete innocence
jokes.explanation: Buster's cheerful obliviousness to the problematic framing of his own statement is pure character comedy. 'Slave buddies' is a phrase only Buster would use, only in this context, with only this level of innocent enthusiasm. Dark but earned through character.

jokes.text: Michael rescues Ann in the church. 'You are Ann, right? Yes. Of course you are.' / 'It is. We got her.' / 'I think we're crashing some poor guy's wake.'
jokes.setup: Michael and company arrive at the church to rescue Ann
jokes.punchline: Michael has to verify it's Ann (the Ann-is-forgettable gag continues); the triumphant 'We got her' as if she were a missing person; Michael's immediate pivot to worrying about the wake they're disrupting
jokes.explanation: The 'You are Ann, right?' extends the Ann-forgettability gag to its logical conclusion — even in a rescue mission, Michael isn't sure which one is Ann. 'We got her' as a rescue declaration for a girl sitting in a church is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: Gob runs up: 'Michael, Michael, Michael! / Fleeing the country. You bastard.' — Michael: 'No. I'm still not fleeing.'
jokes.setup: Gob has followed Michael back to Mexico, still convinced he's fleeing
jokes.punchline: Gob's dramatic accusation ('Fleeing the country. You bastard.') and Michael's weary 'I'm still not fleeing' — the 'still' implies this accusation has become routine
jokes.explanation: Running gag extension — Gob's certainty that Michael is always fleeing. 'You bastard' delivered with genuine outrage is good character work. The 'still' in Michael's denial is the best detail.

jokes.text: Michael to Ann: 'Yeah? Way to plant, Ann.'
jokes.setup: Ann says she was sitting right there the whole time (they didn't notice her when leaving); Michael is mildly exasperated
jokes.punchline: 'Way to plant, Ann' — sarcastically praising her for sitting still so well, also playing on 'plant' as in plant lady from earlier and Ann's general wallflower quality
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Way to plant, Ann' is a masterpiece of economy and layering: it's sarcastic praise for sitting still; 'plant' evokes the office plant lady from the opening; it characterizes Ann as a human wallflower; and it rhymes, making it quotable to the point of memeability. This is the kind of throwaway line that AD makes look effortless. The show later named an episode 'Her?' but 'Way to plant, Ann' might be the funnier Ann dismissal.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I'll be in your car.' Michael adds: 'George Michael, uh, listen. Why don't you and Plant wait in the stair car.' [calling Ann 'Plant']
jokes.setup: Michael continues the Ann rescue logistics conversation
jokes.punchline: Michael now refers to Ann as 'Plant' — the nickname is sticking, and he's deploying it casually as if it's her actual name
jokes.explanation: Quick escalation of joke #62. Michael has already nicknamed Ann 'Plant' and is using it matter-of-factly in front of George Michael. The casual deployment of the nickname is funnier than the coinage itself.

jokes.text: Gob: 'That's his girlfriend? Is she funny or something?' Michael: 'Don't worry about it.'
jokes.setup: Gob sees Ann for the first time; Michael describes her as George Michael's girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Gob's genuine confusion ('Is she funny or something?') — he can't figure out why anyone would date someone so unremarkable, assumes there must be a hidden quality
jokes.explanation: Another beat in the Ann running gag. Gob's question ('Is she funny or something?') tries to find an explanation for Ann's existence as a girlfriend. 'Don't worry about it' is the right non-answer. Consistent, funny, good rhythm.

jokes.text: Gob: 'The only reason I didn't ask you to come is because of your chicken impression. We sure did get in a lot of trouble for that last time.' / Gob starts doing the chicken impression. Michael: 'Hey, no. You're doing it again.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains why he didn't invite Gob; they reference previous Mexico trouble
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately does the Bluth chicken impression while explaining why it causes trouble — he cannot help himself, demonstrating exactly why he wasn't invited
jokes.explanation: The Bluth chicken impression is one of the show's beloved running gags. The joke here has perfect logic: Gob explains the chicken impression gets them in trouble, and then immediately does it, proving Michael's point. 'Hey, no. You're doing it again' is the ideal response. Character-specific, earned, great structure.

jokes.text: Mexican locals attack Gob because of the chicken impression. Gene Parmesan appears: 'Get off him. He doesn't know what he's doing. He's American.' Mexican man: 'Americano, eh? Just like me.'
jokes.setup: Gob does the chicken impression in Mexico; locals react badly (callback to earlier Mexico flashback about inflammatory gestures)
jokes.punchline: Gene defends Gob by saying 'He's American' — meaning this excuses his behavior. The Mexican man's response ('Americano, eh? Just like me') deflates the excuse entirely
jokes.explanation: The callback to the Mexico/inflammatory gesture flashback pays off here. Gene's defense ('He's American') is both xenophobic and useless. The Mexican man saying 'Just like me' — he's also American — completely undercuts Gene's excuse. Subverts the assumption and calls out casual bias simultaneously. Smart comedy.

jokes.text: Gob does his chicken impression — Michael says 'We sure did get in a lot of trouble for that last time.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Gob about why he wasn't invited to Mexico; Michael cites the chicken impression as the reason
jokes.punchline: The callback to the Mexico chicken impression trouble is demonstrated as Gob does it again immediately — suggesting he has learned nothing
jokes.explanation: The chicken impression runner (which spans the whole series) pays off here in context. Gob doing it again despite the clearly stated danger is perfect character comedy. The 'Get off him. He doesn't know what he's doing. He's American.' rescue is an additional laugh.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I went to our mother because I don't have any friends.' Michael: 'Oh, that's funny.' Gob: 'Thank you.'
jokes.setup: Gob confesses to Michael that he has no friends — the arc completes emotionally
jokes.punchline: Michael says 'That's funny' — meaning strange/ironic. Gob takes it as a compliment on his humor and says 'Thank you.' The misunderstanding reveals Gob's desperate need for positive feedback from his brother.
jokes.explanation: The 'Gob has no friends' arc's emotional peak, delivered with perfect economy. 'That's funny' meaning 'how ironic' misread as 'you are amusing' is a wordplay-based character joke that reveals everything about both brothers in three words each. Gob's 'Thank you' — earnest, pleased, completely missing the point — is heartbreaking and hilarious simultaneously. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Not crazy about? Or not crazy about your son growing up?' / Gob: 'Maybe — Maybe you're right about that.' / 'See? You are a friend.' Michael: 'You know, I'm — I'm always gonna look out for you.'
jokes.setup: Michael offers brotherly advice about Ann; the brothers have a genuine bonding moment
jokes.punchline: The bonding is immediately undercut by what follows — Ice appears and tackles Michael (implied), revealing that despite the warm words, real consequences are imminent
jokes.explanation: The sincere brotherly moment is needed for the episode's arc, but the comedy here is lighter. It sets up the physical gag to come. Competent but not the episode's strongest beat.

jokes.text: Gob: 'See? You are a friend.' — Michael nearly has a genuine moment — then Ice tackles Gob from behind
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are having a rare genuine brotherly moment
jokes.punchline: Ice tackles Gob from behind — Michael watches, then admits he saw Ice coming but 'wanted to see where you were going with that'
jokes.explanation: The physical gag of Gob being tackled mid-heartfelt-conversation is funny on its own, but Michael's confession — 'I did see him coming. It's just I kind of wanted to see where you were going with that' — is the real punchline. It reveals Michael's detached, almost scientific interest in Gob's emotional growth. Character-specific and structurally excellent.

jokes.text: Ice tackles Michael. Gob: 'Sorry. I did see him coming. It's just... I kind of wanted to see where you were going with that.'
jokes.setup: Michael and Gob are having a heartfelt brotherly moment; Ice approaches
jokes.punchline: Gob admits he saw Ice coming and deliberately let him tackle Michael to see how the sincere speech would end — choosing curiosity over fraternal protection
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. Gob's admission that he watched Michael about to get tackled and chose to let it happen because he was curious about the speech is the perfect Gob moment — self-interested, slightly sociopathic, but delivered with such casual honesty that it's both shocking and completely in character. 'I kind of wanted to see where you were going with that' is one of the best lines in the episode. Devastating and hilarious.

jokes.text: Michael arranges for Gene and Ice to be Gob's paid friends for the weekend while they look for George Sr.
jokes.setup: Gob has no friends; Michael wants to do something nice for his brother
jokes.punchline: Michael's solution to Gob's loneliness is to pay professionals — a bounty hunter and a P.I. — to hang out with him. Friendship outsourced and monetized.
jokes.explanation: The resolution of the Gob-has-no-friends arc is perfectly in keeping with the show's worldview: the solution is financial. Michael is being generous but in the most transactional way possible. The fact that the P.I. and the bounty hunter are literally the only people available to be Gob's weekend friends is quietly sad and funny.

jokes.text: Michael pays Gene and Ice to be Gob's friends for the weekend while they search for George Sr.
jokes.setup: Michael resolves Gob's no-friends problem
jokes.punchline: Michael's solution is to pay two adult men — a private detective and a bounty hunter — to be Gob's friends for the weekend. Simultaneously kind and devastating.
jokes.explanation: The episode's no-friends arc concludes with Michael purchasing friendship for Gob — which is both the most Bluth solution possible and unexpectedly sweet. The joke lands because it's the logical endpoint of everything that came before.

jokes.text: Michael sits with Ann to get to know her: 'Ann, why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?' Long silence. Reaction beat.
jokes.setup: Michael has resolved to get to know Ann; he sits down with her for a one-on-one conversation
jokes.punchline: The silence — Ann apparently has nothing compelling to say, or Michael immediately regrets asking — is the joke. Inferred from context that this is a long, uncomfortable beat.
jokes.explanation: The visual/silence punchline to the episode-long Ann setup. After all the work to get to know her, the silence says everything. Ann's blankness as a comedic character (later crystallized in 'Her?') is perfectly deployed here. The joke is in what ISN'T said.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Mother!' Lucille: 'Buster!' — Buster has been delivered to their building by Lupe's relatives, thinking he was in Mexico the whole time.
jokes.setup: The narrator reveals Buster is home; Lucille thought he was in Mexico
jokes.punchline: Buster arrives home and is shocked — he thought he was in Mexico. The reveal that his 'Mexican adventure' was a six-minute drive the whole time pays off fully.
jokes.explanation: The full payoff of Buster's Mexico subplot. His return home is framed as an arrival — the horror of discovery landing on both Buster (he never escaped) and Lucille (he never left). Good structural comedy with a satisfying close.

jokes.text: Michael returns to work and rattles off Ann's school details: algebra test, Mr. Mason, Kim Cho cheating. George Michael: 'Wow. You really got to know her.' Michael: 'Well, we did spend three hours together in that car ride.' / 'So, what'd you think?' Michael: 'I don't like her.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is impressed that Michael knows so much about Ann's school life after spending time with her
jokes.punchline: Despite knowing all the practical details, Michael's verdict is simply 'I don't like her' — three hours of logistics have only confirmed his initial gut reaction. The specificity of what he learned (algebra test, cheating classmate) makes the conclusion funnier.
jokes.explanation: The perfect capper to the episode's A-story. The whole episode builds to Michael getting to know Ann, and the payoff is that he knows the most trivial logistical facts about her school life but still simply doesn't like her. 'I don't like her' delivered after all that effort is the ideal button — honest, anti-climactic, and entirely in character. One of the episode's three or four best jokes.

jokes.text: Tag scene: Buster reclaims his hand chair. Then: 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!'
jokes.setup: Buster reclaims his childhood hand chair from Lupe; brief victory
jokes.punchline: 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!' — Buster telling his mother to pleasure herself with the hand chair, delivered with the fury of a child's tantrum but the content of an adult insult
jokes.explanation: An excellent tag. The hand chair callback from the Buster-in-Santa-Ana sequence gets a final, explosive payoff. 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!' is quintessential Buster — weaponizing innocuous childhood furniture into a shocking sexual insult, delivered with the petulance of a six-year-old. Deeply character-specific and completely unexpected. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Teaser: Buster reclaims his hand chair — 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!'
jokes.setup: Buster returns home and reclaims his hand chair from Lupe
jokes.punchline: 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!' — Buster's outrage at Lucille giving away the chair is expressed through what might be the most inadvertently Freudian possible sentence
jokes.explanation: One of the most quotable Buster lines in the series. 'Make love in your own hand, Mother!' is maximally Freudian for a character whose relationship with his mother is the show's central psychological joke. Buster appears to mean 'go sit in your own furniture' but says something far more disturbing. The hand chair foreshadowing of his future hand loss makes this even richer on rewatch. Among the episode's highest-scoring moments for character integration and quotability. Earns 8.9 for the perfection of the accidental psychosexual subtext.

jokes.text: Tag scene: Tobias seeks medical attention. 'Thank God! A new doctor. Finally we can get a straight answer in this place.' Doctor: 'Uh, I've got some bad news.' Then Gene Parmesan appears. 'I'm Gene Parmesan. How you doing?' Lucille: 'He got me again!' Narrator: 'But I did overhear that he's bleeding internally.'
jokes.setup: Tobias needs medical attention (presumably from the blue makeup or some other incident); a doctor enters with bad news
jokes.punchline: Gene Parmesan is the doctor (or has replaced the doctor), does his reveal, gets Lucille's excited reaction ('He got me again!'), and the narrator drily notes that Tobias is bleeding internally as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: A multi-layer tag that fires on every cylinder. Gene's reveal in a medical context is the perfect escalation of his theatrical entrance gag. 'He got me again!' shows Lucille will ALWAYS be thrilled by Gene's reveals. The narrator's afterthought — 'he's bleeding internally' — treats a medical emergency as a minor footnote, the ultimate deadpan dismissal. Perfect AD ending.

tmdb_overview: Michael is increasingly frustrated over being abandoned by the family, and when the staircar is found in Mexico, Michael vows to find his father and give him a piece of his mind. Also, Buster spends time with Lupe's family and thinks he's in Mexico.
insight: Steady callback barrage sustains 81-point episode despite absent standout moments.
